skill.name: spec-workflow
skill.description: Guides implementation work through a spec-driven development workflow. It checks acceptance criteria, supports TDD, limits scope, and coordinates branch and PR steps.
skill.summary: Enforce a spec-first TDD workflow for implementing scoped subtasks.
skill.author: Clionegohan

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution, weak-crypto, C2, and entropy findings are false positives caused by Markdown formatting, example labels, Japanese text, and code examples. The confirmed risk is medium because the skill instructs agents to read specification files, update task status files, and coordinate branch or PR actions in a user project.

medium_findings.title: Workflow Can Read and Update Project Specification Files
medium_findings.description: The skill directs the agent to read subtask files and update subtask or story status files. This is legitimate for a spec workflow, but it can modify project state if used without user review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file read and status update behaviors are explicitly described in the workflow. The intent appears legitimate, but the filesystem side effects require user oversight.

medium_findings.title: Automated Branch and Pull Request Workflow Delegation
medium_findings.description: The skill says it automatically invokes branch and PR workflows after acceptance criteria confirmation and completion checks. These actions are expected for implementation work, but they can affect repository state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow clearly describes branch and PR delegation with user confirmation prompts. This is not malicious, but it is a meaningful operational side effect.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick findings occur in Markdown text, fenced examples, template strings, or command-style skill names. No executable script or shell invocation was found in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens are inside documentation and illustrative TypeScript snippets. There is no real shell execution primitive or bundled executable file.

low_findings.title: Parent Directory References Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The path traversal alerts point to relative links for SPEC_FORMAT.md and WORKFLOW.md. These links may lead an agent outside the skill directory, but they are reference documentation links rather than hidden file access or exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact lines are Markdown links to project documentation. The traversal pattern is real text, but no automatic read or secret access behavior is shown.

low_findings.title: C2 and Weak Crypto Alerts Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The C2 alerts match acceptance criteria labels such as AC2, and the weak cryptography alerts appear to match Markdown or file extension text. No command-and-control behavior or cryptographic implementation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are workflow prose, examples, or AC numbering. I did not find evidence of networking, beaconing, encryption routines, or obfuscation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem State Changes in Workflow Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs agents to update subtask and story files after implementation. Users should review these edits before allowing the workflow to continue.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The status update behavior is explicit and can change repository files. It is not malicious, but it is a concrete side effect.

content.value_statement: Implementation work can drift from accepted requirements and miss tests. This skill keeps work tied to acceptance criteria, TDD, and scoped subtask completion.
actual_capabilities: Reads a subtask specification path before implementation begins.
Prompts for acceptance criteria confirmation before coding work.
Maps acceptance criteria to TDD red, green, and refactor steps.
Flags requests that are outside the current subtask scope.
Checks acceptance criteria before marking work complete.
Coordinates branch and pull request workflow steps after user confirmation.
limitations: It depends on existing EPIC, Story, and Subtask files.
It does not create upstream specifications by itself.
It cannot guarantee tests are complete without project context.
Branch and PR behavior depends on related skills being available.

use_cases.title: Implement a Scoped Subtask
use_cases.description: Use the workflow to read a subtask, confirm acceptance criteria, and implement only the required behavior.

use_cases.title: Keep Feature Work Test First
use_cases.description: Turn accepted criteria into tests before implementation, then refine code after the tests pass.

use_cases.title: Control Scope During Delivery
use_cases.description: Identify requests that are outside the current subtask and ask whether they should become separate work.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Subtask
prompt_templates.scenario: Begin a simple implementation task from an existing specification.
prompt_templates.prompt: Implement subtask 001-01-01. Read the subtask file, show the acceptance criteria, and ask for confirmation before coding.

prompt_templates.title: Use TDD for a Feature
prompt_templates.scenario: Convert acceptance criteria into tests before implementation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the spec workflow for this feature. Derive tests from the acceptance criteria, run the red step, then implement the smallest passing change.

prompt_templates.title: Check Scope Before Adding Work
prompt_templates.scenario: Evaluate whether a requested change belongs in the current subtask.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this extra request against the current subtask acceptance criteria. Tell me whether it is in scope before making changes.

prompt_templates.title: Complete and Prepare Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Finish implementation and prepare handoff for review.
prompt_templates.prompt: Verify each acceptance criterion, confirm tests pass, update the subtask status if appropriate, and prepare the branch and PR workflow prompts.

output_examples.input: Implement a login subtask from the existing specification.
output_examples.output: The assistant presents the acceptance criteria, asks for approval, writes failing tests, implements the required login behavior, and checks each criterion before completion.

output_examples.input: Add CI configuration while working on a formatting subtask.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains that CI configuration is outside the accepted criteria and asks whether it should be proposed as a separate subtask.

output_examples.input: Finish a completed subtask and prepare review.
output_examples.output: The assistant summarizes the satisfied criteria, confirms the test result, updates the task status when approved, and prepares the pull request workflow.

best_practices: Confirm acceptance criteria with the user before editing project files.
Keep each implementation limited to one subtask and one clear outcome.
Review generated branch, status, and PR changes before approving them.
anti_patterns: Starting implementation before the acceptance criteria are visible and approved.
Adding unrelated improvements because they are convenient during the task.
Marking work complete before tests and acceptance criteria checks are finished.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It guides implementation through a spec-first workflow with acceptance criteria checks, TDD steps, and scope control.

faq.question: Does it create specifications?
faq.answer: No. It expects upstream EPIC, Story, and Subtask files to already exist.

faq.question: Can it edit files?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow includes reading specification files and updating subtask or story status files after review.

faq.question: Does it run shell commands?
faq.answer: No executable shell command was found in the skill file. The detected command patterns are Markdown and example text.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it can guide file updates and repository workflow actions that need user review.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
